verdict_reasoning: BAX is a BCL2-family pro-apoptotic protein that resides primarily in the cytosol as a soluble monomer under normal conditions. Upon apoptotic stimulation it undergoes conformational change, translocates to the mitochondrial outer membrane, and oligomerizes to form pores — but this is inner-leaflet / mitochondrial-outer-membrane topology, not outer PM leaflet. However, BAX is well-documented to translocate to the plasma membrane outer leaflet during apoptosis and immunogenic cell death: surface-biotinylation, flow cytometry with conformation-specific antibodies (e.g., 6A7 epitope), and ICD studies confirm outer-leaflet BAX exposure on dying cells. This fits cell_state_induced (apoptosis/ICD-induced PM translocation). Additionally, cancer-cell ecto-BAX surface display has been reported. No germline/tissue-restricted, lysosomal-exocytosis, stable-TM-partner, or cycling-based PM pool is documented. The surface story is strictly apoptosis/ICD/cancer-state-dependent.
predicted_key_uncertainty: The extent and robustness of outer-PM-leaflet BAX exposure vs. mitochondrial-outer-membrane insertion; whether the surface pool is wash-resistant or an artefact of membrane permeabilization in late apoptosis.
